category_summary:
**Andrej Karpathy**'s autoresearch experiment dominated the AI conversation — an autonomous agent [found ~20 improvements](/?date=2026-03-10&category=social#item-60c2474f773d) to nanochat over two days, cutting 'Time to GPT-2' by 11%. This sparked broad discussion about AI self-improvement loops, with **Harrison Chase** (**LangChain**) [building an "autoresearch for agents"](/?date=2026-03-10&category=social#item-c7e2c9480ead) variant.

- **Anthropic** [shipped **Claude Code Review**](/?date=2026-03-10&category=social#item-bd415a590ae7), a multi-agent PR review system; engineer **bcherny** reported 200% productivity gains and [explained how separate context windows](/?date=2026-03-10&category=social#item-df876e0caf00) make subagent review effective
- **Anthropic** [filed two lawsuits](/?date=2026-03-10&category=social#item-6ac36a330b78) against the US government alleging retaliation for refusing to remove Claude's restrictions on autonomous lethal warfare — a major AI policy flashpoint
- **OpenAI** announced [acquiring **Promptfoo**](/?date=2026-03-10&category=social#item-d0ed7194746a) for agentic security testing, while **Andrew Ng** [launched **Context Hub**](/?date=2026-03-10&category=social#item-c1afbf0b804e) to combat coding agents hallucinating outdated API docs

A strong contrarian thread emerged around agent reliability: **svpino**'s [viral post declaring](/?date=2026-03-10&category=social#item-3c4a137042ba) "these agents don't work as promised" drew 668K views and resurfaced concrete failures from **Chevrolet**, **Air Canada**, and others. **Ethan Mollick** noted that six weeks after **Claude Cowork** launched, [no competitor has emerged](/?date=2026-03-10&category=social#item-9b4c25eba867) — a telling signal about the gap between lab claims and shipped products.

items.title: Three days ago I left autoresearch tuning nanochat for ~2 days on depth=12 model. It found ~20 chang...
items.content:
Three days ago I left autoresearch tuning nanochat for ~2 days on depth=12 model. It found ~20 changes that improved the validation loss. I tested these changes yesterday and all of them were additive and transferred to larger (depth=24) models. Stacking up all of these changes, today I measured that the leaderboard's "Time to GPT-2" drops from 2.02 hours to 1.80 hours (~11% improvement), this will be the new leaderboard entry. So yes, these are real improvements and they make an actual difference. I am mildly surprised that my very first naive attempt already worked this well on top of what I thought was already a fairly manually well-tuned project.

This is a first for me because I am very used to doing the iterative optimization of neural network training manually. You come up with ideas, you implement them, you check if they work (better validation loss), you come up with new ideas based on that, you read some papers for inspiration, etc etc. This is the bread and butter of what I do daily for 2 decades. Seeing the agent do this entire workflow end-to-end and all by itself as it worked through approx. 700 changes autonomously is wild. It really looked at the sequence of results of experiments and used that to plan the next ones. It's not novel, ground-breaking "research" (yet), but all the adjustments are "real", I didn't find them manually previously, and they stack up and actually improved nanochat. Among the bigger things e.g.:

- It noticed an oversight that my parameterless QKnorm didn't have a scaler multiplier attached, so my attention was too diffuse. The agent found multipliers to sharpen it, pointing to future work.
- It found that the Value Embeddings really like regularization and I wasn't applying any (oops).
- It found that my banded attention was too conservative (i forgot to tune it).
- It found that AdamW betas were all messed up.
- It tuned the weight decay schedule.
- It tuned the network initialization.

This is on top of all the tuning I've already done over a good amount of time. The exact commit is here, from this "round 1" of autoresearch. I am going to kick off "round 2", and in parallel I am looking at how multiple agents can collaborate to unlock parallelism.
https://t.co/WAz8aIztKT

All LLM frontier labs will do this. It's the final boss battle. It's a lot more complex at scale of course - you don't just have a single train. py file to tune. But doing it is "just engineering" and it's going to work. You spin up a swarm of agents, you have them collaborate to tune smaller models, you promote the most promising ideas to increasingly larger scales, and humans (optionally) contribute on the edges.

And more generally, *any* metric you care about that is reasonably efficient to evaluate (or that has more efficient proxy metrics such as training a smaller network) can be autoresearched by an agent swarm. It's worth thinking about whether your problem falls into this bucket too.
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Building on yesterday's [Reddit](/?date=2026-03-09&category=reddit#item-788baa54cb16) discussion of autoresearch, Karpathy's major post: autoresearch agent autonomously found ~20 improvements to nanochat over 2 days, reducing 'Time to GPT-2' by 11%. Improvements included fixing attention scaling, regularization, attention bandwidth, AdamW betas, weight decay, and initialization. He predicts all frontier labs will adopt agent-driven optimization and envisions multi-agent collaboration for research at scale.
items.reasoning: Landmark post from one of AI's most respected practitioners. Over 1M views, 11K likes. Demonstrates concrete, validated results of AI agents autonomously improving neural network training. Predicts a fundamental shift in how ML research is done. Deeply technical yet accessible.

items.title: I'm excited to announce Context Hub, an open tool that gives your coding agent the up-to-date API do...
items.content:
I'm excited to announce Context Hub, an open tool that gives your coding agent the up-to-date API documentation it needs. Install it and prompt your agent to use it to fetch curated docs via a simple CLI. (See image.)

Why this matters: Coding agents often use outdated APIs and hallucinate parameters. For example, when I ask Claude Code to call OpenAI's GPT-5.2, it uses the older chat completions API instead of the newer responses API, even though the newer one has been out for a year. Context Hub solves this.

Context Hub is also designed to get smarter over time. Agents can annotate docs with notes — if your agent discovers a workaround, it can save it and doesn't have to rediscover it next session. Longer term, we're building toward agents sharing what they learn with each other, so the whole community benefits.

Thanks Rohit Prsad and Xin Ye for working with me on this!

npm install -g @aisuite/chub

GitHub: https://t.co/OCkyxXQMCq

Argues multiple uncorrelated context windows help catch bugs similarly to how different engineers catch each other's mistakes. Predicts agents will eventually write perfect bug-free code.
items.reasoning: High engagement (2774 likes, 98K views), from Anthropic engineer, provides substantive technical insight into why multi-agent code review works—separate context windows overcome shared blind spots. Important theoretical framing.

continuation.original_title: There's a common assumption in AI right now that if one language model can do a task reasonably well...
continuation.reference_text: Echoing yesterday's **Social** discussion on multi-agent systems

items.summary_html: Echoing yesterday's Social discussion on multi-agent systems, bcherny (Anthropic) explains how test-time compute and separate context windows make subagents effective for code review. Argues multiple uncorrelated context windows help catch bugs similarly to how different engineers catch each other's mistakes. Predicts agents will eventually write perfect bug-free code.
items.content_html:
@Rahll 👋 Roughly, the more tokens you throw at a coding problem, the better the result is. We call this test time compute.

One way to make the result even better is to use separate context windows. This is what makes subagents work, and also why one agent can cause bugs and another (using the same exact model!) can find them. In a way, it’s similar to engineers — if I cause a bug, my coworker reviewing the code might find it more reliably than I can.

In the limit, agents will probably write perfect bug-free code. Until we get there, multiple uncorrelated context windows tends to be a good approach.

items.content:
"Build a company that benefits from the models getting better and better" — @sama

devin brain uses a couple dozen modelgroups and extensively evals every model for inclusion in the harness, doing a complete rewrite every few months. hearing a lot of "devin is good now" feedback but its largely the same process that the team has been running since @ScottWu46 bet on cloud agents in November 2023. agents are really, really working now and you had to have scaled harness eng + GTM to prep for this moment

items.content:
loved this from @karpathy

over the weekend I built "autoresearch but for agents"

Same idea — give an AI coding agent your agent code + an eval dataset, let it experiment autonomously overnight. It modifies the code, runs evals via LangSmith, keeps improvements, discards regressions. You wake up to a better agent.

Bring your own agent (any framework or none), dataset, and eval metrics.

https://t.co/CnBgVbWKEz
items.author: @hwchase17

items.title:
Some of the stories they aren't telling you:

• Chevrolet's chatbot sold a car for $1
• Air Canada h...
items.content:
Some of the stories they aren't telling you:

• Chevrolet's chatbot sold a car for $1
• Air Canada had to honor a refund policy that its chatbot made up
• A pipeline ran 20x over cost for 6 days without anyone noticing

People didn't realize because nothing broke. There were no crashes and no alerts.

That's the issue with agentic applications.

They always generate something that looks coherent and don't raise any suspicion unless it's too late.

There's an amazing free YouTube lecture and blog post from @arshdilbagi that will help you fix this with a practical framework.

Here is what you'll learn:

• How to set up end-to-end trace instrumentation
• How to build alerts around a silent failure taxonomy
• An eval system built from production data
• Complete and concrete implementation steps

Every section of the blog ends with exactly what to do next.

items.title: I still give the book Understanding Deep Learning by Simon J.D. Prince a good recommendation, but ch...
items.content:
I still give the book Understanding Deep Learning by Simon J.D. Prince a good recommendation, but chapter 21: Deep learning and Ethics was sloppy. It could have been a chapter to really dig in on case studies, but it was just the basic public news story level coverage of bias and such, like:

“In AI, it can be pernicious when this deviation depends on illegitimate factors that impact an output. For example, gender is irrelevant to job performance, so it is illegitimate to use gender as a basis for hiring a candidate. Similarly, race is irrelevant to criminality, so it is illegitimate to use race as a feature for recidivism prediction.”

If they had stuck with “illegitimate”, then it would have been a question of societal choices, but “irrelevant” is a question about data, and your priors shouldn’t be so strong that data can’t move them.

I would like to see a book or course walk through a machine learning problem with the input features being presented as something like car choices: color, style, doors, horsepower, etc. Do lots of analysis over representation, training, and generalization, then swap the feature labels to socially charged ones.

What makes generalization credible in one situation but not the other?
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack critiques the ethics chapter (Ch. 21) in 'Understanding Deep Learning' by Simon Prince, arguing the treatment of bias/fairness is sloppy. He challenges the conflation of 'irrelevant' and 'illegitimate' regarding features like race/gender in ML, and proposes a pedagogical exercise: present ML features as car attributes, do analysis, then swap labels to socially charged ones to examine when generalization is credible.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful, substantive critique from a highly respected technologist about ML ethics pedagogy. The proposed exercise is genuinely novel and thought-provoking. Good engagement (284 likes). Touches on the deeply relevant tension between statistical validity and social values in ML.

items.content_html:
I still give the book Understanding Deep Learning by Simon J.D. Prince a good recommendation, but chapter 21: Deep learning and Ethics was sloppy. It could have been a chapter to really dig in on case studies, but it was just the basic public news story level coverage of bias and such, like:

“In AI, it can be pernicious when this deviation depends on illegitimate factors that impact an output. For example, gender is irrelevant to job performance, so it is illegitimate to use gender as a basis for hiring a candidate. Similarly, race is irrelevant to criminality, so it is illegitimate to use race as a feature for recidivism prediction.”

If they had stuck with “illegitimate”, then it would have been a question of societal choices, but “irrelevant” is a question about data, and your priors shouldn’t be so strong that data can’t move them.

I would like to see a book or course walk through a machine learning problem with the input features being presented as something like car choices: color, style, doors, horsepower, etc. Do lots of analysis over representation, training, and generalization, then swap the feature labels to socially charged ones.

What makes generalization credible in one situation but not the other?

items.title: The frontier AI field seems pretty set, and it is unlikely to add new players until LLM-centric prog...
items.content:
The frontier AI field seems pretty set, and it is unlikely to add new players until LLM-centric progress starts to plateau. If/when that happens the market will change a lot.

On the LLM side, two unknowns: Can Meta re-enter the race? Can xAI return to and stay at the frontier?

items.title:
Claude Code just turned every PR into a multi-agent bug hunt.

Anthropic shipped Code Review, a new ...
items.content:
Claude Code just turned every PR into a multi-agent bug hunt.

Anthropic shipped Code Review, a new Claude Code feature that auto-deploys a swarm of agents the moment a pull request opens.

Tested internally on most of their own PRs, results speak:

> PRs with real review comments: 16% to 54%
> Less than 1% of findings flagged as wrong by engineers
> 1,000+ line PRs: 84% surface bugs, avg 7.5 issues each

Here's the actual mechanism: agents run in parallel, each hunting independently. They then cross-verify findings before surfacing them, cutting noise.

You get one clean summary comment plus inline flags.

PR review used to be the bottleneck when AI ships 5-10x more code than before. Now any Team or Enterprise user can plug this into their pipeline and get senior-level depth on every merge, automatically, at

$15-25 per review.
https://t.co/muUeSATtzT

items.title:
Datacenter networking performance is a pressing challenge as modern workloads continue to grow. 

In...
items.content:
Datacenter networking performance is a pressing challenge as modern workloads continue to grow. 

In our latest ADRS blog post, we show how we use the OpenEvolve framework to automatically discover improved congestion control algorithms, evolving from a SOTA baseline to a strategy that reduces queue length by 49% on the NSDI ’22 PowerTCP benchmark.

items.summary: Ion Stoica (UC Berkeley prof, Databricks co-founder) shares ADRS blog post about using OpenEvolve framework to automatically discover improved datacenter congestion control algorithms, achieving 49% queue length reduction vs SOTA PowerTCP benchmark.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical contribution from a highly credible researcher. Demonstrates AI/LLM-driven algorithm discovery applied to datacenter networking - a novel and practical application of evolutionary AI methods. Moderate engagement.

items.title: The "how does AI improve individual productivity" discussion is much less interesting than "how does...
items.content: The "how does AI improve individual productivity" discussion is much less interesting than "how does AI improve organizational efficiency?" We have a lot of answers to the former, but it will always get eaten by the latter if we don't experiment with new approaches to organizing.

Which techniques let us find secret knowledge in an LLM?

The challenge is finding realistic evaluations. But Chinese LLMs present a great, realistic challenge - can we extract censored knowledge? The models know it, but lie/mislead.

We benchmark many approaches from prior work

Man, I don't know what type of circus you've worked on, but in my 20+ years of professional career, I've never worked with people as dumb and as unreliable as AI.

Brilliant one day. Completely retarded the next one.

items.title: It seems it was unfortunate that companies lumped every concern about AI into the overall labels of ...

It creates a giant tangle around discussions of the risks and rewards of AI use cases, and often centralizes decisions that should be distributed.

items.title:
Cursor and VS Code just lost their biggest edge over students.

Zed launched a free Pro plan for any...
items.content:
Cursor and VS Code just lost their biggest edge over students.

Zed launched a free Pro plan for any university student or teacher.

Here's what's included for 12 months:
> Full Pro feature access
> $10/month in LLM token credits
> Unlimited AI edit predictions

Edit predictions are the core mechanic worth understanding. Zed's open-source model, Zeta, predicts your next *edit*, not just the next token.

Change one element in a repeated structure, and it propagates the same edit across the pattern automatically.

The editor itself runs on Rust with GPU acceleration, no Electron layer between you and the output. You can also plug in Claude, Gemini, or local Ollama models using your own API keys.

If you've been locked out of AI-assisted coding tools by a $20/month paywall, that barrier is now gone.

A full agentic coding environment, free, while you're still learning.
https://t.co/Du2rL3zlU9
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Zed editor launched free Pro plan for students/teachers, including AI edit predictions via their open-source Zeta model, challenging Cursor and VS Code

items.title: This is great, but you need to be careful not to treat this as a complete solution for verifying you...
items.content:
This is great, but you need to be careful not to treat this as a complete solution for verifying your code.

The system that proposes a solution shouldn't be the one trying to find its flaws.

Same model, same training biases, same blind spots.

items.summary: svpino warns that the system proposing code solutions shouldn't be the one verifying them—same model, same biases, same blind spots. Appears to be responding to Claude Code Review announcement.

items.content_html:

The system that proposes a solution shouldn't be the one trying to find its flaws.

Same model, same training biases, same blind spots.

items.title:
I am honored to be joining the Scientific Advisory Board of the @UN https://t.co/gk0cz7dT4y

The Sec...
items.content:
I am honored to be joining the Scientific Advisory Board of the @UN https://t.co/gk0cz7dT4y

The Secretary-General's Scientific Advisory Board and its associated global network of scientific institutions advise UN leaders on breakthroughs in science and technology – how to harness the benefits of these advances and mitigate potential risks. The Board provides independent insights on trends at the intersection of science, technology, ethics, governance and sustainable development to support UN System organizations in anticipating, adapting to and leveraging scientific advancements in their work for people, planet and prosperity.

items.title: @NewmoonCap maybe. See the quote from the Eli Lilly guy in my substack yesterday, though: current AI...
items.content:
@NewmoonCap maybe. See the quote from the Eli Lilly guy in my substack yesterday, though: current AI is really not good at biological reasoning. “AI is far from curing cancer and most other diseases.] If you just ask them to solve biology or chemistry questions, they’re not particularly good at it… They’re trained on the human language, not on the language of chemistry, physics, and biology.”

Maybe future AI, probably based on different principles, might be better.

items.title:
Stop hiring designers. One URL now generates your entire brand campaign.

Google Labs and DeepMind j...
items.content:
Stop hiring designers. One URL now generates your entire brand campaign.

Google Labs and DeepMind just expanded Pomelli to 170+ countries. It builds your brand's marketing content from scratch.

> Free, no waitlist
> Social posts, product photos, animated videos
> Powered by Veo 3.1 and Nano Banana
> No brand kit needed, just a URL

items.title: If you wouldn't let a student grade their own exam, you shouldn't let your AI coding assistant verif...
items.content:
If you wouldn't let a student grade their own exam, you shouldn't let your AI coding assistant verify its own code.

The system that proposes a solution shouldn't be the one trying to find its flaws.

Same model, same training biases, same blind spots.

If Claude misunderstands your requirements when writing the code, it will misunderstand them when reviewing it.

You want a reviewer who challenges the generator's intent.

Code generation and code verification are fundamentally different problems.

This is a single point of failure for code quality.

Here is an alternative:

• Use specialized verification tools to review the code
• Have the agent write tests first, then write the code to pass them
• Manually review the code
• Ask the agent to list what could break before accepting the code
• Use a linter or static analysis tool as a deterministic check
• Ask a different model to critique the code
• Ask the agent to explain its decisions

I've been working with Qodo for quite some time. They specialize in ensuring your code works. Their agentic PR review achieves the highest precision and recall in issue finding on the market.

They sponsor my work, and gave me a coupon code for 1 complete month free of their TEAMS plan:

Coupon Code: UNBIASED (all caps)

Enjoy!

items.title: We built a neat tool that lets you convert a directory of Powerpoint files into clean, structured ma...
items.content:
We built a neat tool that lets you convert a directory of Powerpoint files into clean, structured markdown - that Claude Code / agent SDK / any generalized agent wrapper can easily understand.

The pptx skill in Claude Code is quite basic and doesn’t have high-fidelity understanding over graphics/charts/tables.

Our project Surreal Slides uses LlamaParse to convert presentations into clean structured data that you can put into a db (@SurrealDB) for simple retrieval, without having to take screenshots of the data on the fly.

Thanks to @itsclelia for this project, check it out: https://t.co/Fj1PASv8IP

items.summary: jerryjliu0 (LlamaIndex) announces a tool to convert PowerPoint files to structured markdown for use with Claude Code and agent SDKs, using LlamaParse and SurrealDB. Notes Claude Code's native pptx handling is basic.

Our project Surreal Slides uses LlamaParse to convert presentations into clean structured data that you can put into a db (@SurrealDB) for simple retrieval, without having to take screenshots of the data on the fly.

items.content_html:
@Afinetheorem Daniel Kahneman was there too, as well as @erikbryn, but @Ph_Aghion couldn't make it, IIRC.

It took place at the same time as the CIFAR Neural Computation and Adaptive Perception workshop, which is why Geoff Hinton, Yoshua Bengio and I showed up.

I learned so much that I decided to attend the whole event.

items.title: It's like if we're struggling to solve a math problem, but making no progress. Perhaps we never woul...

items.title:
The reason this happens is almost always the same:

Most agents are black boxes. You give them a tas...
items.content:
The reason this happens is almost always the same:

Most agents are black boxes. You give them a task, they disappear, and you get a result (or you don't). You can't see what they are doing while they are doing it.

I'm currently looking into Sai. They are a pretty cool agentic assistant that combines all of the following:

• Claude skills
• Terminal
• Browser
• MCP tools
• Desktop GUI for computer use

Sai does a couple of things really well:

1. First, it's designed from the ground up to ask for approval before doing anything important.

2. And the agent has its own dedicated Windows workspace, and you can watch it work live and jump in at any point.

Another interesting thing:

Because Sai operates on a full desktop, not just a browser or an API, it can handle applications that block standard automation. That opens up a whole class of tasks that most agents can't touch.

It runs in the background while you keep working. You can manage it from any device through a browser. And it keeps going even when you're not watching.

I think Sai is doing the right thing by pitching an always-on coworker that lets you see everything, intervene anytime, and run on a full desktop.

You can get a 7-day free trial at https://t.co/VO0cLbu8TN

First 1,000 users get founder pricing at $20/month.

Thanks to the Sai team for collaborating with me on this post.

items.title:
The other big shift?

Owning and unifying the entire stack.

Enterprises don’t need another science ...
items.content:
The other big shift?

Owning and unifying the entire stack.

Enterprises don’t need another science experiment.
They need the easy button to operationalize AI.

That means:

→ Silicon
→ Optics
→ Systems
→ Orchestration layers

Working as one cohesive platform, not stitched together across 5 vendors and 10 roadmaps.

Fragmentation kills momentum.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon argues enterprises need unified AI stacks (silicon to orchestration) rather than fragmented multi-vendor approaches.
items.reasoning: Substantive point about AI infrastructure consolidation, part of a Cisco-sponsored thread but contains real strategic insight.

items.content: RE: https://dair-community.social/@Meron/116199326654385711As someone who came to the US as a refugee with political asylum I can't overstate how cruel this is. I was already terrified and had nightmares every day. And here they're telling you that you didn't get to safety after all that, that they will reverse your status on a whim, if they deem it safe for you to return. As Warsan Shire wrote, no one leaves home unless home becomes the mouth of a shark.

items.title:
The bigger opportunity is APIs.

We saw 4G enable Uber, Grubhub, the app economy.

Now 5G + network ...
items.content:
The bigger opportunity is APIs.

We saw 4G enable Uber, Grubhub, the app economy.

Now 5G + network APIs enable developers to build applications that assume guaranteed performance.

That is a fundamental shift.

When performance becomes programmable, telcos move up the value chain.

From connectivity providers → to experience platforms.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon argues 5G network APIs represent a fundamental shift, enabling telcos to move from connectivity providers to experience platforms.
items.reasoning: Infrastructure perspective relevant to AI/edge computing deployment, but primarily a telecom business take.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon argues 5G network APIs represent a fundamental shift, enabling telcos to move from connectivity providers to experience platforms.

items.title:
Rakesh said something that stuck with me:

“AI infrastructure is about building a massive computer w...
items.content:
Rakesh said something that stuck with me:

“AI infrastructure is about building a massive computer with huge amounts of network bandwidth.”

Read that again.

AI isn’t just compute.

It’s:

→ Extreme east-west traffic
→ Massive bandwidth demands
→ Tight coupling between silicon, optics, systems, and orchestration

If your network is an afterthought, your AI strategy is too.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon emphasizes AI infrastructure requires massive network bandwidth, citing Cisco SVP on east-west traffic demands.
items.reasoning: Technical insight about network requirements for AI workloads. Sponsored but contains useful framing about infrastructure bottlenecks.

Premium connectivity will redefine ARPU, but only if product and marketing teams stop selling speed and start selling experiences.

items.title:
5G network slicing is the unlock.

It allows operators to create purpose-built slices of the network...
items.content:
5G network slicing is the unlock.

It allows operators to create purpose-built slices of the network for specific use cases:

→ Ultra-low latency for gaming
→ High uplink quality for live streaming
→ Secure, fraud-protected transactions for retail
→ Immersive AR and VR experiences

This is not theoretical.

Operators in Asia are already monetizing event-based passes, four-day upgrades, even influencer packages.

items.title:
We are watching a shift from selling bandwidth → to selling outcomes.

Speed is table stakes.

Perfo...
items.content:
We are watching a shift from selling bandwidth → to selling outcomes.

Speed is table stakes.

Performance is premium.

Think about a World Cup stadium:

→ Ordering food from your seat
→ Streaming live as an influencer
→ Placing secure mobile payments
→ Playing real-time AR experiences

In those moments, latency and reliability matter more than raw Mbps.

That is where differentiated connectivity comes in.
